event_blocks.summary: The World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series 2023 Men's Standings are presented, highlighting New Zealand's five podium finishes and two gold medals, Argentina's second-place position with a Vancouver win, and France's Olympic qualification. Fiji and Australia are noted as rounding out the automatic Olympic places with three rounds remaining after Hong Kong. The narrative shifts to a vibrant stadium atmosphere, with Fijian Fans waving large blue flags and wearing team colors, including blue wigs and face paint, amidst cheers, while the commentator acknowledges the significant support for both teams and declares Hong Kong the 'Mecca of Rugby Sevens' and 'Fiji's second home,' where Fiji aims for a 20th title against the All Black Sevens. A New Zealand flag is waved, and a floral banner is displayed within the lively crowd. A dramatic, smoky, black-and-white montage of rugby players in action—running, tackling, and celebrating—accompanies an energetic, anthemic rock track with male vocals, culminating in a close-up of a New Zealand player in an 'All Blacks Sevens' jersey as the text 'NEW ZEALAND' emerges from smoke, underscored by lyrics about fighting for victory.

Fijian Fans.description: Enthusiastic supporters in the stadium, waving large blue Fijian flags, wearing blue wigs, face paint, and team apparel, actively cheering and celebrating, contributing to the 'big support' noted by the commentator.

New Zealand.description: Team ranked first in the 2023 Men's Standings with 120 points and a +705 point differential, having been on the podium five times this season with two gold medals; later shown in a dramatic black-and-white montage with a player wearing a black 'All Blacks Sevens' jersey featuring Adidas branding, and explicitly named as 'the All Black Sevens' by the commentator.

Australia.description: Ranked fifth in the 2023 Men's Standings with 94 points and a +220 point differential, and noted by the commentator as rounding out the automatic Olympic places for the Olympics.

France.description: Ranked third in the 2023 Men's Standings with 95 points and a +124 point differential, and noted by the commentator as already qualified for the Olympics.

Fiji.description: Ranked fourth in the standings with 94 points and a +550 point differential, and noted by the commentator as rounding out the automatic Olympic places; fans are shown waving large blue Fijian flags, wearing blue wigs, and cheering enthusiastically in the stadium, with the commentator highlighting Hong Kong as 'Fiji's second home' where they aim for a 20th title.

Argentina.description: Ranked second in the 2023 Men's Standings with 108 points and a +284 point differential, and identified by the commentator as winners in Vancouver.

World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series.description: Branded prominently on the standings graphic, indicating the tournament context held in Hong Kong, which the commentator describes as 'the Mecca of Rugby Sevens'.

New Zealand Fans.description: Shown waving a New Zealand flag in the stands, dressed in team colors, contributing to the lively atmosphere and the 'big support' mentioned by the commentator.

Rugby Players (Montage).description: Appearing in a high-contrast, smoky, black-and-white sequence, shown running, tackling, and celebrating, all set to an energetic, anthemic rock track with male vocals; one player is clearly identifiable in a black 'All Blacks Sevens' jersey with Adidas logo.

Stadium Crowd.description: A diverse group of spectators in a stadium setting, actively engaged, waving flags, and holding banners, including one with a floral design and text 'Just Be', with their enthusiastic background noise audible throughout the commentary.

raw_segments: The video opens with a stunning nighttime aerial view of Hong Kong’s illuminated skyline, reflecting off the water, setting a vibrant and global tone. As background crowd noise is audible, this transitions to a graphic displaying the '2023 Men's Standings' for the World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series. A commentator details the standings, noting "New Zealand five times they've been on the podium this season, two gold medals, they led the way," followed by "Argentina in second, winners in Vancouver," and "France already qualified for the Olympics." He adds that "Fiji and Australia" are "rounding out those automatic Olympic places," with three rounds left after Hong Kong. The scene then shifts to energetic crowd shots of fans in a stadium, waving large blue Fijian flags and wearing team colors, some with blue wigs and face paint, cheering passionately, while the commentator remarks on the "big support for both teams." He emphasizes that Hong Kong "is the Mecca of Rugby Sevens, and it's Fiji's second home," where they "will go for a 20th title all time" when they face "the All Black Sevens." A woman waves a New Zealand flag, and another holds a banner with a floral design amidst the lively stadium atmosphere. The video cuts to a dramatic, smoky, black-and-white montage of rugby players in action—running, tackling, and celebrating—all set to an energetic, anthemic rock track with male vocals. This culminates in a close-up of a New Zealand player in a black jersey with 'All Blacks Sevens' and Adidas branding, followed by the bold text 'NEW ZEALAND' emerging from smoke, as the music continues with lyrics like "away from a victory hour" and "If you want it then you'll fight."

event_blocks.summary: The World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series Men's Cup final in Hong Kong commenced with a high-contrast black-and-white montage of rugby players from New Zealand and Fiji, set to an energetic rap track. This transitioned to a wide shot of a packed Hong Kong Stadium, buzzing with fans waving flags and press staff present. A male commentator introduced the event as the "Sunday night Super Session, the Men's Cup final," confirming Fiji and New Zealand as the finalists after "incredible men's semi-finals." The narrative then detailed New Zealand's 12-7 victory over France, securing their place in the final, and Fiji's dramatic golden-point try against Great Britain to also advance. The 'ROAD TO THE FINAL' graphic was displayed, highlighting both teams' unbeaten records in Hong Kong. An upbeat pop/rock song, 'I'm Gonna Be (500 Miles),' played as aerial shots showed the diverse, cheering crowd, with fans in vibrant attire and team jerseys. The atmosphere intensified with fireworks signaling the Men’s Cup Final, and on-screen graphics displayed team rosters and coaches, including Fiji captain Tavita Dangunu and New Zealand coach Clark Laidlaw. Pre-game rituals included drummers in dragon-themed shirts creating smoke effects as Fiji and New Zealand walked onto the pitch for the "30th Sevens World Series Cup final," a match with "huge history" between the two nations. Following the national anthems, with the Fiji anthem sung by an Anthem Singer and the New Zealand anthem performed in Māori, the teams lined up for the highly anticipated gold medal match.

Fijian Fans.description: Enthusiastic supporters in the stadium, waving large blue Fijian flags, wearing blue wigs, face paint, and team apparel, actively cheering and celebrating, as their team, the fourth-ranked Fiji, prepares to face New Zealand in the Men's Cup Final, with captain Tavita Dangunu and players Moisamoa Nandolavo and Talatholo mentioned.

New Zealand.description: Players in black 'All Blacks Sevens' jerseys with Adidas branding, standing in formation, arms linked, listening intently and reverently to the powerful choral rendition of their national anthem, "God Defend New Zealand," with one player having a white bandage on his nose and another placing his hand over his heart, sweat visible on their faces, eyes closed or looking upward in solemn respect, visibly moved by the patriotic lyrics.

Hong Kong Cityscape.description: An aerial view of Hong Kong’s skyline and harbor, with modern skyscrapers, bridges, and waterways, overlaid with the text 'HONG KONG', establishing the event’s location.

Fiji.description: Players in white jerseys with black and blue traditional patterns, 'FIJI AIRWAYS' branding, standing in formation, singing their national anthem with solemn expressions and heads tilted upward, sweat visible on their faces, accompanied by the powerful voice of the Anthem Singer.

World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series.description: Branded prominently on the field-side banners and digital screens, indicating the tournament context, with the event taking place in Hong Kong as noted on the signage, visible throughout the stadium.

Fan in Red and White Jersey.description: A close-up of a fan in a red and white jersey, shouting with excitement, capturing the raw emotion and energy of the crowd.

New Zealand Fans.description: Shown waving a New Zealand flag in the stands, dressed in team colors, contributing to the lively atmosphere, as their team, the number one side, prepares to face Fiji in the Men's Cup Final.

Rugby Players (Montage).description: Appearing in a high-contrast, smoky, black-and-white sequence, shown running, tackling, and celebrating; one player is clearly identifiable as New Zealand's Narohe McGarvey-Black in a black 'All Blacks Sevens' jersey with Adidas logo, whose winning moment is described by the commentator.

Stadium Crowd.description: A diverse and enthusiastic crowd in the stands, visible in the background, now quieted and observing the solemn national anthem ceremony, with sponsor branding like 'CATHAY', 'HSBC', and 'DHL' along the field perimeter.

Press Staff.description: Individuals wearing bright yellow vests labeled 'PRESS', seen interacting with fans and capturing the event, indicating media presence at the stadium.

Fan with 'Just Be' Banner.description: A spectator holding a banner with the text 'Just Be' and floral design, adding a personal and artistic touch to the crowd's display.

Stadium Fireworks.description: Bright fireworks bursting above the stadium field, illuminating the night sky and signaling the start of the final match.

Drum Performers.description: Performers in orange and black dragon-themed shirts, energetically striking large drums, creating billowing smoke effects that fill the field and enhance the dramatic entrance, with their powerful, rhythmic drumming sound explicitly noted by the commentator as accompanying the teams' entrance.

raw_segments: The minute opens with a dramatic, high-contrast black-and-white montage of rugby players, including a New Zealand player in an 'All Blacks Sevens' jersey and a Fiji player in a 'FIJI AIRWAYS' jersey, running, tackling, and celebrating with intense energy. This visual intensity is underscored by an upbeat, energetic rap track with a male vocalist, featuring lyrics about power, victory, and not losing. The montage culminates with 'NEW ZEALAND' and 'FIJI' text, followed by the 'SUPER SESSION' logo and 'WORLD RUGBY HSBC SEVENS SERIES' branding. The music then transitions to a dramatic orchestral sound as the scene shifts to a wide shot of a packed Hong Kong Stadium, capturing the vibrant energy of a diverse crowd, including fans waving Fijian and New Zealand flags, cheering, and holding phones. Press staff in yellow vests are seen, along with a man in an 'All Blacks' shirt with a child. An aerial view of the Hong Kong cityscape and harbor, overlaid with 'HONG KONG', is then shown as a male commentator begins, describing the event as "Our Sunday night Super Session, the Men's Cup final" and an "all singing dancing Hong Kong Sevens." He expresses excitement to be back for a "full Sevens Series event" at "Hong Kong Stadium," confirming "Fiji, New Zealand in the final." The commentator then references "incredible men's semi-finals" earlier in the day, mentioning "France against..." as the video returns to stadium action, showing a powerful tackle and a close-up of an excited fan in a red and white jersey shouting, capturing the raw emotion of the event.

The video opens with a high-contrast, black-and-white montage of intense rugby action, featuring a New Zealand player in a black 'All Blacks Sevens' jersey, later identified by the commentator as Narohe McGarvey-Black, running and tackling with fierce determination. The commentator details McGarvey-Black's 'winning moment' for New Zealand, securing a 12-7 victory over France and propelling them 'through to the big show.' The scene transitions to a vibrant, full-color stadium environment where the same New Zealand player celebrates a try, arms raised, as teammates rush to congratulate him amidst erupting crowd cheers and New Zealand fans waving flags. The focus then shifts to Fiji, where a player in a white jersey with 'FIJI AIRWAYS' and traditional Fijian patterns scores a dramatic try in extra time, diving over the line as defenders attempt to stop him. The commentator describes Fiji's journey, noting they 'had their chances to win it in regulation time' against 'brilliant defense from Great Britain,' before going to 'golden point' where 'Fiji found a way right on sudden death, just in time,' securing their spot in the 'Cup Final.' Fijian fans, wearing blue wigs and face paint, wave large blue flags and cheer wildly, their excitement adding to the audible stadium roar. The video then cuts to a wide shot of the illuminated stadium at night, before transitioning to a 'ROAD TO THE FINAL' graphic for the World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series in Hong Kong. The commentator confirms both New Zealand and Fiji are 'unbeaten here in Hong Kong this weekend,' noting New Zealand's 'tough draw' against South Africa, as the graphic displays their tournament progression, including semi-final, quarter-final, and pool stage results, all set against an electric atmosphere of crowd noise and commentary.

The segment opens with a male commentator's voice detailing the 'Road to the Final' graphic for the World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series in Hong Kong, explaining how Fiji, having overcome South Africa, and Argentina, winners in Vancouver, progressed to the final match against New Zealand. As the commentary concludes, an upbeat pop/rock song, 'I'm Gonna Be (500 Miles)' by a male vocalist, starts playing, setting a festive tone. The visuals transition from the graphic to wide aerial shots of the packed stadium, filled with an enthusiastic and diverse crowd. Close-ups reveal spectators in vibrant attire—some shirtless, others in floral shirts or team jerseys—cheering, waving flags, and holding drinks. A fan in a black shirt with '23' and a circular logo proudly waves a large blue and white flag, while another in a red and white jersey shouts with excitement, embodying the electric atmosphere. Fijian and New Zealand fans are seen waving their respective flags, contributing to the lively celebration, with fans dancing and taking photos. Large screens above the stands display 'Hello Hong Kong' and 'South Stand', alongside sponsor logos like HSBC and Cathay Pacific, reinforcing the event's location and global, festive nature, all amplified by the continuous background music.

The minute opens with an energetic close-up of a fan in a blue sailor costume, complete with hat and sunglasses, holding an inflatable guitar, amidst a buzzing crowd. The camera pulls back to reveal a packed Hong Kong Stadium, alive with an electric atmosphere as fans, including enthusiastic Fijian and New Zealand supporters, wave flags, cheer, and raise their arms. Colorful attire, face paint, wigs, and hats, like a sparkly-brimmed black hat or a bright orange wig, add to the festive mood, with a man in a tiger-print costume dancing energetically. Upbeat, rhythmic music pulses through the stadium, accompanied by the roar of the crowd. A commentator describes the "sights and sounds of Hong Kong Stadium on Sunday night," noting the "wonderful week" enjoyed by "expats, visitors, [and] loads of locals" at full capacity. The scene transitions to a wide shot under bright lights as fireworks burst above the field, signaling the start of the Men’s Cup Final. On-screen graphics confirm the World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series in Hong Kong, detailing the high-stakes match between Fiji and New Zealand, displaying team rosters, player names like Fiji captain Tavita Dangunu, Moisamoa Nandolavo, and Talatholo, along with coaches Ben Gollings for Fiji and Clark Laidlaw for New Zealand, as players warm up on the field.

The minute opens with a detailed on-screen graphic displaying the team rosters for the World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series Men’s Cup Final in Hong Kong, listing players and coaches for Fiji and New Zealand. A male commentator's voice is heard, highlighting New Zealand players Koli, Molia (the co-captains), and young Cody Vait, noting his debut tournament start in a cup final. The scene then transitions to wide shots of the packed stadium at night, illuminated by bright floodlights, showcasing the expansive field and enthusiastic crowd, whose murmurs and cheers are audible in the background. The commentator continues, setting the scene for the gold medal match before introducing Olympic silver medalist and former England and GB captain, Tom Mitchell, asking him about the players' current feelings. Tom Mitchell's voice is then heard, describing the intense atmosphere of a final, where "it's enough to get your heart rate pumping, sweaty palms and some tears in the eyes." As close-ups reveal fans in vibrant attire—including a man in a sombrero and poncho, a a woman with glitter face paint, and a young man in a Jordan cap recording on his phone with a red UAG case—a stadium announcer's deep, resonant voice begins to boom over the loudspeakers, though largely unintelligible amidst the rapidly building, massive roar of the crowd. The energy culminates as a group of performers in orange and black dragon-themed shirts energetically strike large drums, creating smoke effects that billow across the field, their powerful and distinct drumming sounds resonating with the continued crowd cheers, adding to the dramatic pre-game atmosphere under the glow of stadium lights and sponsor banners.

The video opens with a dramatic pre-game atmosphere at a packed stadium under bright floodlights, where thick plumes of white and blue smoke billow across the field, accompanied by the powerful, rhythmic drumming from Drum Performers dressed in vibrant orange and black dragon-themed shirts. As the smoke clears, the Fiji team, wearing white jerseys with black and blue traditional patterns and 'FIJI AIRWAYS' branding, walks confidently onto the green pitch, followed by the New Zealand team in their black 'All Blacks Sevens' jerseys. A male commentator announces, 'Fiji and New Zealand drummed on to Hong Kong Stadium,' describing their 'slow walk, pre-anthems,' for what is the '30th Sevens World Series Cup final.' He highlights the 'huge history' between these 'pre-eminent nations in World Rugby Sevens,' noting they've played over a hundred knockout games since the series kicked off in 1999. The stadium is alive with cheering fans, visible in the stands waving flags and banners, under dynamic spotlights, with prominent sponsor logos like 'Cathay', 'Carlsberg', and 'HSBC' lining the perimeter. The commentator emphasizes this is 'the blue ribbon event of them all, the one they want to win the most: the Hong Kong Sevens Cup final.' He adds that Fiji 'made the final in November' but 'lost to Australia' this season, while for New Zealand, 'it's been a while' since their last final appearance, setting an electric mood charged with anticipation.

The minute opens with a wide-angle view of a packed stadium at night, brilliantly lit by floodlights, as two rugby teams, Fiji in white jerseys and New Zealand in black, walk onto the pitch. A commentator's voice provides context, recalling New Zealand's 2014 cup final win against Tom Mitchell's England side, before announcing a pause for the national anthems, initially expecting New Zealand's first, then Fiji's. However, a stadium announcer's clear voice then instructs the diverse and enthusiastic crowd, now quieted, to stand for the national anthem of Fiji. The teams line up facing each other in the center of the field, flanked by officials, under the watchful eyes of the respectful crowd and prominent sponsor banners for 'Cathay', 'HSBC', 'DHL', and 'World Rugby'. A man in a black suit and white shirt steps forward, holding a microphone, and begins to sing the Fiji national anthem. The camera zooms in on his focused expression as solemn, orchestral music plays, followed by his strong male voice singing the lyrics, "Blessing grant oh God of nations, on the aisles of Fiji..." The atmosphere is deeply respectful, capturing the ceremonial gravity of this World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series final in Hong Kong.

The video captures a deeply emotional and solemn moment during the pre-match national anthem ceremony at the World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series final in Hong Kong. The Fiji players, clad in their distinctive white jerseys with black and blue traditional patterns and 'FIJI AIRWAYS' branding, stand in formation on the brightly lit field. Their faces, seen in alternating close-up and wide-angle shots, are etched with intense focus, solemn concentration, and subtle smiles, reflecting the gravity and honor of the occasion. With heads tilted upward and mouths open, they are heard singing their national anthem, a powerful and reverent vocal performance that fills the stadium. The lyrics, clearly audible, speak of unity, freedom, and glory, directly referencing 'Fiji' multiple times and reinforcing the deep cultural and national significance of the moment for the team. The camera pans across players like 'TALACOLO' and 'MURANDA', emphasizing their collective pride as their voices ring out. The stadium crowd remains subdued, observing the ceremony, while sponsor banners for 'CATHAY', 'HSBC', and 'Dettol' line the field, underscoring the global scale of the event.

The video opens with a close-up of Fiji players in their white jerseys, adorned with black and blue traditional patterns and 'FIJI AIRWAYS' branding, standing solemnly with heads tilted upward as a powerful male voice, the Anthem Singer, fills the stadium with their national anthem. The camera captures intense close-ups of individual players, revealing their focused and emotional expressions, including one with sweat glistening on his face and another with a beard, eyes closed in reverence. As the Fiji anthem concludes with a powerful final note, the stadium erupts in applause and cheers. The camera transitions to a wide aerial shot, showing both teams—Fiji in white and New Zealand in black 'All Blacks Sevens' jerseys—lined up opposite each other, with the Anthem Singer in a black suit standing between them. An announcer's clear voice then instructs, "Ladies and gentlemen, please remain upstanding for the national anthem of New Zealand." Orchestral music swells, introducing the New Zealand national anthem, which the Anthem Singer performs with deep concentration in Māori. Wide shots show the New Zealand team standing in formation, arms linked, facing the crowd, with the stadium lights illuminating the packed stands and sponsor banners like 'CATHAY', 'HSBC', and 'DHL' visible along the field. The atmosphere is solemn and charged with anticipation as the anthem continues, the players remaining still, heads bowed or eyes closed, absorbing the moment before the match begins.

The video captures a solemn and emotionally charged moment before a rugby match, as the New Zealand 'All Blacks Sevens' team stands in formation on a well-lit field, clad in their black Adidas and ALTRAD sponsored jerseys. A powerful, choral rendition of "God Defend New Zealand," their national anthem, fills the stadium, creating a reverent atmosphere. The camera alternates between wide shots of the entire team and intimate close-ups, highlighting players with focused and respectful expressions, sweat glistening on their faces, eyes closed or tilted upward in solemn reverence. One player places his hand over his heart, while others maintain a stoic, contemplative demeanor, visibly moved by the patriotic lyrics such as "God of nations at thy feet" and "God defend our free land." The packed stadium crowd is quieted in observation, underscoring the gravity and tradition of this pre-game ritual for the World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series event in Hong Kong, with digital scoreboards displaying '1st Half' and '0-0' and sponsor branding like 'HSBC', 'CATHAY', and 'DHL' visible along the perimeter.

event_blocks.summary: The New Zealand 'All Blacks Sevens' team, identifiable by their black jerseys and a player with a bandaged nose, stood with arms linked and solemn respect during their national anthem, sweat visible under stadium lights, while the Fiji team, in white jerseys with traditional patterns and 'FIJI AIRWAYS' branding, sang their anthem with heads tilted upward and eyes closed, accompanied by a male choir. A wide aerial view showcased the packed stadium under bright floodlights, featuring 'World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series' branding, as a commentator highlighted the intense emotion "pouring out of the players" for the cup final between New Zealand and Fiji in Hong Kong. Referee AJ Jacobs, officiating his 52nd match and wearing a red 'Emirates Fly Better' shirt, adjusted his earpiece, with commentators noting his role in the final. The Fiji team concluded their anthem, with leader Molala guiding pre-kick-off breathing exercises, before a sharp whistle and a shouted "in!" preceded the referee tossing the rugby ball. Fiji players leaped, with one securing the ball mid-air amidst a celebratory huddle, met by a loud whistle and the crowd's roar and "whoop" as the match officially commenced under the 'World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series' banner, with 'Hong Kong' signage visible on the field.

Stadium Crowd.description: A diverse and enthusiastic crowd in the stands, visible in the background during the anthem, now quieted and observing the ceremony, then erupting in roaring applause and cheers after the anthems, and again with a loud roar and 'whoop' as the match begins. Sponsor branding like 'CATHAY', 'HSBC', and 'DHL' is visible along the field perimeter.

Referee.description: AJ Jacobs, wearing a red shirt with 'Emirates Fly Better' on the back, adjusting his earpiece, identified on-screen as officiating his 52nd match in the series. Commentators explicitly state that 'the responsibility is with AJ Jacobs to take care of this final.' A sharp whistle sound is heard as he prepares to start the match.

New Zealand.description: Players in black 'All Blacks Sevens' jerseys with Adidas branding, standing in formation, arms linked, listening intently to the anthem, one player with a white bandage on his nose, another placing his hand over his heart, sweat visible on their faces, eyes closed or looking upward in solemn respect. Commentators note their participation in a fifth cup final this series, having won in Sydney and Los Angeles.

World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series.description: Branded prominently on the field-side banners and digital screens, indicating the tournament context, with the event taking place in Hong Kong as noted on the signage. Commentators refer to this as a 'cup final' and mention New Zealand's history in the series.

Fiji.description: Players in white jerseys with black and blue traditional patterns, 'FIJI AIRWAYS' branding, standing in formation, singing the national anthem with solemn expressions and heads tilted upward, eyes closed in reverence, accompanied by a powerful male choir. Commentators mention their leader, Molala, guiding them through pre-kick-off breathing exercises as part of their routine, and they are seen leaping to secure the ball at the start of the match.

event_blocks.summary: The national anthems of Fiji and New Zealand commence, featuring close-ups of players from both teams, Fiji in white jerseys with traditional patterns and 'FIJ AIRWAYS' branding, and New Zealand in black 'All Blacks Sevens' jerseys with Adidas branding, their expressions solemn, some with eyes closed or looking upward, sweat glistening, and hands over hearts. A commentator emphasizes Fiji's extensive rugby history, noting their familiarity with the event and their 19 all-time wins, 8 in the professional era, while also mentioning Kaisamuri Vai's tournament debut and Tom Mitchell. An emotional older woman in the crowd is observed wiping tears. As the anthems conclude, a commentator reflects on the sport's emotional power and passion, the upcoming 14 minutes of play, and the dream of playing in the Hong Kong final. The scene shifts to a wide aerial view of the stadium, showcasing the lined-up teams, the crowd, and sponsor banners like 'HSBC', 'CATHAY', and 'DHL', with the 'World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series Hong Kong' logo visible. Following a bright white flash and a close-up of a focused New Zealand player with a bandage on his nose, the crowd's anticipation culminates in a sharp whistle, signaling the start of the Men's Cup Final between New Zealand and Fiji. The game kicks off with a wide shot, a Fiji player preparing to kick and referee AJ Jacobs, in a red 'Emirates Fly Better' shirt, adjusting his earpiece. Intense action ensues immediately with a tackle by a New Zealand player on a Fiji player. The scoreboard confirms 'CUP FINAL', 'FIJ 0 - NZL 0', '1st Half 6:55', as a commentator notes New Zealand going 'long off the kick-off' and names 'Battere Renga'.

Referee.description: AJ Jacobs, wearing a red shirt with 'Emirates Fly Better' on the back, adjusting his earpiece, identified on-screen as officiating his 52nd match in the series. A sharp whistle from the referee signals the start of the match.

New Zealand.description: Players in black 'All Blacks Sevens' jerseys with Adidas branding, standing in formation, arms linked, listening intently to the anthem, one player with a white bandage on his nose, another placing his hand over his heart, sweat visible on their faces, eyes closed or looking upward in solemn respect. Commentators declare the match as 'New Zealand and Fiji for Hong Kong glory' in the 'Men's Cup Final,' noting New Zealand going 'long off the kick-off.'

Scoreboard Graphic.description: On-screen display in the top left corner showing 'CUP FINAL', 'FIJ 0 - NZL 0', '1st Half 6:55', indicating the match status and time, with team flags and scores clearly visible. This is confirmed by commentators as the 'Men's Cup Final' at kick-off.

Fiji.description: Players in white jerseys with black and blue traditional patterns, 'FIJI AIRWAYS' branding, standing in formation, singing the national anthem with solemn expressions and heads tilted upward, sweat visible on their faces. event_blocks.summary: In the World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series Cup Final in Hong Kong, New Zealand secured a 24-17 victory over Fiji. The match began with a scoreless tie before New Zealand's Cody Vai scored the opening try, followed by a conversion, making it 7-0. New Zealand extended their lead to 10-0 with a try from Sione Molia, and further tries from Rokolisoa and Molia, coupled with conversions, pushed their advantage to 24-7 by halftime, despite a TMO review confirming Rokolisoa's try and another for McGarvey-Black. Fiji showed resilience in the second half, with Naduvale scoring a try and Talafolo adding another, narrowing the gap to 24-17. However, New Zealand's defense held firm, and despite a sin-binning for A. Nicole, they managed possession in the final minutes, with Rokolisoa scoring a final try to seal the win. Post-match, captain Sione Molia expressed his team's elation at ending a nine-year drought for a Hong Kong title, highlighting the youth and energy of the squad. Dylan Collier was named HSBC Player of the Final for his outstanding performance. The victory propelled New Zealand to the top of the 2023 Men's Standings with 142 points, edging closer to Olympic qualification.

A male commentator sets the scene, questioning if the "All Blacks Sevens" can "win gold here in Hong Kong" after their "cracking start" and current "24-7" lead. New Zealand players in black 'All Blacks Sevens' jerseys advance the ball, with the commentator noting their regained possession. Visually, New Zealand's Rokolisoa (number 4) sprints powerfully down the wing, evading Fijian defenders in white 'FIJI AIRWAYS' jerseys. He is tackled near the try line, but the ball is offloaded to a teammate who dives for the try. Referee AJ Jacobs, in a red 'Emirates Fly Better' shirt, signals for a TMO review, which confirms the try, and he raises his arm to signal the score amidst the intense crowd cheering. The commentator highlights New Zealand's "extraordinary defensive effort," having "only conceded six tries coming into this final." The scene then shifts to a close-up of Rokolisoa preparing for a lineout, holding the ball above his head, with the commentator observing that "Rokolisoa is going to take his time here and hits his mark." The scoreboard updates to 6:22, and an on-screen graphic notes Fiji has conceded just one second-half try this weekend. The commentator also mentions Leroy Carter "wrestling Nanduvallo over the sideline" and consistently "bringing the good stuff for New Zealand," reinforcing the dynamic and physical nature of the match, all set against the backdrop of sponsor banners for HSBC, Cathay, DHL, and Capgemini.

The World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series Cup Final between Fiji and New Zealand unfolds under stadium lights, with the scoreboard showing New Zealand leading 24-7 in the second half. The segment captures New Zealand, in their black 'All Blacks Sevens' jerseys, executing a fast-paced offensive play, passing the ball quickly as commentators mention players like Cook Savage, Collier, Molia, and Carter, under the guidance of head coach Clark Laidlaw. During this intense period, New Zealand player Ngaroa McGarvey-Black (number 6) is seen exerting significant physical effort and is later assisted off the field by medical staff, indicating a possible injury. However, Fiji, in white jerseys with traditional patterns, swiftly win a crucial steal at the breakdown, with the commentator identifying the player as Wulaka, number 88. The crowd erupts in cheers as Fiji launches a rapid counter-attack, with the commentator excitedly naming players like Moana, Nanduvalo, Longanai Marci, Joseva Talafolo, and Beterereinga as they advance downfield. The intensity peaks as Bukayaro finds a way through the defense, prompting a surge of excitement from the commentator and the engaged crowd, who are seen in colorful attire, including a woman dancing and a man in a blue sailor costume. Referee AJ Jacobs, in his red 'Emirates Fly Better' shirt, actively monitors the play and signals for a restart, while the 'World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series' branding is visible on the field. The atmosphere remains highly competitive and festive, with Fiji desperately seeking to close the 24-7 gap.

The World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series Cup Final between Fiji and New Zealand is in its second half, with the scoreboard initially showing Fiji 7, New Zealand 24. The action begins with a New Zealand player, Rokolisoa (number 4), sprinting down the wing with the ball, pursued by a Fijian defender. A commentator exclaims, "What a tackle that is. Amanaki Nicole," as Rokolisoa is tackled near the try line. Despite the tackle, the ball is passed to a teammate who dives to score. The stadium crowd erupts in enthusiastic cheers and celebration, with fans in colorful attire—including a man in a dinosaur costume and a woman in a blue and white floral shirt—cheering loudly. The scoreboard updates to Fiji 12, New Zealand 24, confirming a try for Fiji. Commentators confirm Fiji scored, with one identifying "Talafolo" as the scorer, stating, "Fiji score first in the second half and they absolutely needed to." A replay then shows the player, identified in the visual context as A. Nicole (number 9) of New Zealand, breaking through the defense and grounding the ball. Commentators, including Sean Mitchell, praise the "passage of play," noting a "line break" and mentioning "Bukayaro" in the build-up, marveling at the strength to get over the line. The scene shifts to a wide-angle view of the field, highlighting the dynamic movement of players and the vibrant stadium atmosphere, with sponsor banners for HSBC, DHL, and Cathay visible. The video concludes with a close-up of the scoring player, Nicole, running with the ball, followed by a shot of the scoreboard confirming the try and the updated time of 4:11 remaining in the second half. The energy is electric, with fans waving flags and celebrating the moment, while referee AJ Jacobs in a red shirt oversees the play.

The segment opens with a close-up of an exhausted Fiji player, sweat glistening on his face, wearing a white jersey with traditional patterns and 'FIJI AIRWAYS' branding. The scoreboard shows Fiji trailing New Zealand 12-24 in the 2nd half with 4:11 remaining, as a commentator notes, "Conversion doesn't make it. 24-12. We got a ball game, folks," and highlights Fiji's historical success, stating, "No team has won more cup finals here than Fiji." The scene transitions to a wide shot where Fiji's number 94, Naduvale, stands at the center of the field, preparing for a lineout and executing a high, precise pass to a teammate. The commentator reveals that New Zealand's A. Nicole, number 9, has been sent to the sin bin, leading to "bad news for New Zealand, they're down to six, a power play for Fiji." The camera follows the ball as New Zealand's A. Nicole receives it and sprints downfield, evading defenders before being tackled near the try line by a Fijian defender. A ruck quickly forms, intensifying with players from both teams, including Fiji's number 94 and New Zealand's Rokolisoa (number 4), actively engaging. The referee, AJ Jacobs, in a red shirt, closely monitors the physical struggle, signaling for a review, while the commentator notes, "Play on says the referee" regarding a loose ball incident and a potentially high tackle on Vata Rerenga. The scoreboard updates to 3:58, with the score remaining 12-24. The packed stadium, filled with an enthusiastic crowd and sponsor banners like 'CATHAY', 'HSBC', and 'DHL', adds to the tense and competitive atmosphere, as the commentator reiterates, "New Zealand playing with six here."

The second half of the Men’s Cup Final of the World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series between Fiji and New Zealand unfolds with New Zealand leading 24-12, as the scoreboard graphic confirms the 'CUP FINAL' status and the remaining time ticks down, with commentator 1 noting "3:25 to go" and "12 points the difference." The action begins with a tackle near the sideline, where New Zealand's Brady Rush (number 24) is brought down by a Fijian defender. A referee's whistle signals a stoppage, and commentator 1 announces that Nathungu wins the steal for Fiji. Fiji's Nanduvallo and Longaname Masi are involved in the play, with commentator 1 highlighting Talatholo as "the danger man" who is "so hard to contain" before the ball goes forward off Longaname Masi, prompting commentator 2 to add that the player "just couldn't see the ball coming his way." New Zealand regains possession, with A. Nicole (number 9) sprinting downfield, evading defenders before being tackled near the try line. Rokolisoa (number 4), explicitly named by commentator 1, then sprints down the wing and is tackled just short of the line. Another referee's whistle is heard, and commentator 2 notes a "high tackle" as New Zealand is awarded a penalty, with commentator 1 identifying Sioni Molia and mentioning Te Paea Cook-Savage won't be in a hurry to take it. Commentator 2 observes the tempo slowing down, while later noting Fiji missed an opportunity with Talatholo "crying out for someone to come around the corner." The stadium is packed with an enthusiastic crowd, their continuous cheers and roars creating an intense atmosphere, amplified by sponsor banners like DHL, HSBC, Cathay, and Bupa. The sequence ends with a close-up of a New Zealand player (number 24) with the ball, preparing for the next phase, following a "what a kick!" from New Zealand, as the crowd remains engaged and animated.

The second half of the World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series Cup Final between Fiji and New Zealand unfolds with the score at FIJ 12 - NZL 24 and just over two minutes remaining, as the clock ticks down from 2:30. The stadium is alive with a diverse crowd, including fans in colorful attire like a woman in a blue and white floral shirt and a man in a blue sailor costume, while sponsor logos like 'CATHAY' and 'HSBC' adorn the field. Commentary highlights the "skillset on the youngsters" and the "presence of mind" of players, referencing past performances of New Zealand players like Xavier Taito Harris and Rhodes Featherston, and recalling Cody Vai at the anthems. As the action intensifies, New Zealand's A. Nicole (number 9), recently "out of the sin bin," receives the ball. Commentators initially misidentify him as "Rokolisoa" before calling him "the rocket man" as he sprints downfield, evading Fijian defenders with a "big dummy" move. He's eventually tackled near the try line, leading to a ruck involving New Zealand's Rokolisoa (number 4) and a Fijian defender. The play is quickly halted as the commentators confirm the ball "has gone forward," resulting in a Fiji scrum with "two minutes to go." The referee, AJ Jacobs, actively oversees the game, while the on-screen graphics display New Zealand's 'SERIES TRY SOURCE' statistics. The intense sequence concludes with a close-up of a woman in the crowd, her hands clasped in anticipation, reflecting the high stakes of the final moments.

The tense second half of the World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series Cup Final in Hong Kong unfolds with Fiji trailing New Zealand 12-24, 1:46 remaining, as displayed on the scoreboard. The action begins with a ruck near the sideline, where New Zealand's A. Nicole (number 9) and Rokolisoa (number 4) in black 'All Blacks Sevens' jerseys engage physically with Fiji players like Naduvale (number 94) in white. Referee AJ Jacobs, in his red 'Emirates Fly Better' shirt, is actively involved, calling out "Look up!", "Let's go!", and "Back to you!" as the play develops. Commentary notes a "good power play from New Zealand point of view." Jacobs continues to direct, shouting "Coach!", "Five!", and "Set!" before a scrum. With "90 seconds to go," commentary emphasizes that "Fiji need to score, preferably convert it before that clock goes to zero." The play transitions to open field as A. Nicole receives the ball, sprinting downfield, evading defenders. Commentary highlights a "heavy tackle coming in from Brady Rush" and the desire for him to "emulate the feats of his dad Eric here in Hong Kong." The stadium crowd, packed and enthusiastic with flags and sponsor banners like HSBC and Cathay, cheers loudly. A. Nicole is tackled near the try line, with commentary exclaiming, "Oh, what hands from Naduvalo!" as Nicole attempts to strip the ball away. Jacobs is heard questioning, "My Samoa, against who? I tackle against who?" as the play continues. Commentary follows the ball through "Volavola" to "Talafolau," with Jacobs asking, "Is it more than a penalty?" before Rokolisoa is put on the floor, and the ball goes loose, prompting Jacobs to call "Up!"

The segment opens with referee AJ Jacobs, in his bright orange 'Emirates Fly Better' jersey, running across the field as a commentator announces, "Time off says the referee." Jacobs checks a play, with commentators noting a "high tackle against nine black, penalty only," and identifying the player as "Amanaki Nicole nine black." The scoreboard displays FIJ 12 - NZL 24 with 50 seconds remaining in the second half, and a commentator highlights New Zealand's long wait, stating, "New Zealand haven't won a title here in Hong Kong since 2014." The camera widens to show the packed stadium as New Zealand players in black 'All Blacks Sevens' jerseys advance the ball. With 40 seconds left, the commentator emphasizes Fiji's dwindling time, noting, "They're running out of time Fiji, they're at the wrong end of the pitch." A. Nicole (number 9) receives the ball and sprints downfield, evading defenders, prompting an excited commentator to exclaim, "My Samoa gets away from one and another!" The play intensifies as Fiji players, including Naduvale (number 94), attempt to tackle. As the clock ticks down to 25 seconds, the commentator declares, "It's now or never for Fiji." The ball is passed to Rokolisoa (number 4), who sprints down the wing, identified by the commentator as "Talafolo," and is tackled near the try line by a Fijian defender, the camera capturing the physicality. The scoreboard updates to 0:28, then 0:16, with New Zealand maintaining possession. In the final moments, a New Zealand player, again identified as "Talafolo" and "Joseph Talafolo," dives to ground the ball near the try line, and the referee signals the try amidst the excited crowd. On-screen graphics confirm the match context, including New Zealand's long wait for a Hong Kong Cup Final win, as sponsor logos like HSBC, Cathay, and DHL mark the electric field.

The final moments of the World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series Cup Final unfold with the scoreboard showing Fiji 17, New Zealand 24, and the clock at 0:00. The stadium is electric, filled with a vibrant crowd waving flags and cheering wildly, their loud enthusiasm a constant backdrop. A commentator highlights a previous "individual effort" by Fiji's Yoseva Talatolo, who "has thrown them a lifeline," and mentions a conversion attempt, but the focus quickly shifts. Referee AJ Jacobs signals the end of the match, raising his hand to confirm the result. New Zealand players in black 'All Blacks Sevens' jerseys, including A. Nicole and Rokolisoa, erupt in emotional celebration, hugging and embracing, visibly exhausted. The commentator declares, "New Zealand are Hong Kong Sevens champions!", noting, "Look what it means to them," and adding that "nine years is a long time" for them not to win here, having "beaten their great old rivals Fiji." Sione Molia, identified as "the captain," is seen celebrating with teammates and receiving a hug from head coach Clark Laidlaw. In contrast, Fijian players in white 'FIJI AIRWAYS' jerseys, like Naduvale, appear dejected, with one player kneeling on the field under the bright stadium lights, surrounded by sponsor banners. The commentator continues to praise New Zealand's "performance, built on a first half where they were..." as the victorious team basks in their hard-won triumph.

The World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series Cup Final concludes with New Zealand's 24-17 victory over Fiji, as confirmed by the scoreboard graphic. New Zealand players, including A. Nicole (number 9), Rokolisoa (number 4), and Sione Molia (number 27), are seen celebrating intensely on the brightly lit field, hugging and jumping with visible joy and relief. Commentators praise their "ruthless, clinical" performance, calling it an "outstanding" and "well and truly deserved win," also highlighting "Godzemaru Vai" as "absolutely brilliant in the Cup Final." In stark contrast, Fiji players, like Naduvale (number 94), appear dejected, with one player kneeling, head down, reflecting their disappointment. The commentators highlight Fiji's devastation, mentioning "the tears during the anthems" and noting that for Fiji, who consider this their "second home," it was "a bridge too far tonight." They also discuss a tactical point, explaining how Fiji *could* have forced a golden point scenario by not taking a conversion and opting for a restart, though acknowledging "a lot would have had to happen" for it to work. The electric atmosphere is filled with crowd noise and cheering, while sponsor banners like 'CATHAY', 'DHL', and 'Carlsberg' line the field. Medical staff are briefly seen assisting Ngaroa McGarvey-Black (number 6) off the field, indicating a possible injury amidst the emotional release of victory and defeat.

The segment opens with New Zealand 'All Blacks Sevens' players, including A. Nicole (number 9) and Rokolisoa (number 4), celebrating intensely after their 24-17 victory over Fiji, a win a commentator describes as earning them a 'gold medal in Hong Kong' and 'stuff of dreams.' The scene transitions to a post-match interview where captain Sione Molia (number 27), visibly sweaty with a white bandage on his nose, speaks into a microphone. Interviewer Rob Vickerman introduces Molia, highlighting the significance of the win as New Zealand's first title since 2014. Molia, with a serious and reflective expression, states he is 'speechless,' emphasizing that it's been a 'really long time ago' since the team last 'stamped their mark here in Hong Kong,' and that this achievement was something they had been working towards. He praises the team's youth and their energy, expressing pride in their performance. On-screen graphics display Molia's impressive match statistics: 2 tries, 15 carries, and 6 offloads. Molia further reflects on the unique atmosphere of Hong Kong, acknowledging past 'dreadful losses' there, which makes this victory even more significant.

The segment opens with a close-up of Sione Molia, a New Zealand player in a black 'All Blacks Sevens' jersey, visibly sweaty with a white bandage on his nose, giving a post-match interview. He speaks into a 'World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series' microphone, expressing that he was initially in disbelief about the 24-7 halftime score but knew the team had a game plan they executed. A male interviewer congratulates him on a 'great shift' and jokes about keeping the bandage on. The scoreboard in the top left corner confirms 'FULL TIME' with New Zealand victorious (NZL 24 - FIJ 17). The scene transitions to game highlights, showing A. Nicole (number 9) sprinting with the ball, evading a Fijian defender, and diving to score a try, which the TMO confirms. Teammates, including Rokolisoa (number 4), celebrate with intense emotion. A wide shot captures the Hong Kong stadium, filled with a cheering crowd and sponsor banners. The video returns to Sione Molia, now against a red 'World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series' backdrop. A male commentator announces "Sione Molia" and praises other New Zealand players like Leroy Carter, Nadohi Magavi, and Brady Rush, highlighting his status as a "Hong Kong Sevens champion." The commentator then sets up the "HSBC Player of the Final" award. Amidst crowd cheers, a lower-third graphic identifies '5. DYLAN COLLIER - HSBC PLAYER OF THE FINAL', which a second male commentator confirms, praising Collier's "outstanding" performance throughout the game, despite Sione Molia still being on screen.

The segment opens with a close-up interview of Sione Molia, a visibly sweaty New Zealand player in a black 'All Blacks Sevens' jersey, speaking into a tournament-branded microphone against a red 'World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series' backdrop. Despite a lower-third graphic misidentifying him as '5. DYLAN COLLIER - HSBC PLAYER OF THE FINAL', Molia, speaking in a calm, slightly breathless voice, reflects on the game, stating they "started really well" and put Fiji "under a lot of pressure," acknowledging the "Fijian boys they can come back from anything." He emphasizes the team's effort to "keep building pressure" and their luck in managing to "hang out there" for the full 14 minutes. The interviewer then praises their defense, noting they conceded only six tries, to which Molia adds they were "scrambling quite a bit" but the "effort the boys showed was just unreal." As Molia speaks, a man in a red 'ALTRAD' polo shirt, introduced by the interviewer as "Brian Abana," approaches and presents Molia with a clear, faceted trophy. The interviewer then declares, "Unscarred. What an effort. Dylan Collier," further reinforcing the visual/audio discrepancy regarding the trophy recipient. The scene transitions to a wide aerial shot of the stadium at night, filled with a celebratory crowd and illuminated by stadium lights, while a commentator's voice introduces the '2023 MEN'S STANDINGS'. A large graphic overlay confirms New Zealand at #1 with 142 points, followed by Argentina, Fiji, and France, alongside a scoreboard in the top left showing the final score: Fiji 17, New Zealand 24, all under the 'HONG KONG' event location and World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series logo. Throughout the trophy presentation, crowd cheering is audible, contributing to the celebratory atmosphere.

The video opens with a wide-angle night view of the Hong Kong Stadium, brightly illuminated, showcasing the 2023 Men's Standings for the World Rugby HSBC Sevens Series. Commentators immediately highlight New Zealand's dominant position, stating they "have extended their lead at the top, edging towards automatic Olympic qualification and the series title." The graphic prominently displays New Zealand at #1 with 142 points, followed by Argentina, Fiji, and France, as the commentators detail the competitive landscape, noting "Argentina still in second, Fiji in third, France already at their home Olympics, so South Africa as things stand, are the other automatic Olympic qualifiers at the moment." They continue, mentioning "Australia just two points behind, Samoa another three points back, Ireland, USA," emphasizing that "It's going to go down to the wire this season in terms of who books their place in Paris without having to go to regionals." The discussion also covers "Spain, the big story as well, of course, getting out of that relegation bracket for the first time this season." As the camera transitions to the field, players and staff gather near the center, prompting the commentator to observe, "So the players will gather." A closer view captures the New Zealand team, in their black 'All Blacks Sevens' jerseys, including A. Nicole (number 9) and Rokolisoa (number 4), celebrating with intense emotion. In the background, a commentator identifies "Terry Wright... with the grey hair, brilliant winger in his time for the All Blacks," praising his contributions. The scene then widens to show teams lined up for the trophy presentation under a large 'CATHAY PACIFIC HONG KONG SEVENS' arch, with the stadium lights and cheering crowd creating a vibrant, celebratory post-match atmosphere.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins with an aerial view of the brightly lit Hong Kong Stadium during the Cathay/HSBC Hong Kong Sevens, showcasing sponsor banners. Teams, including New Zealand in black 'All Blacks Sevens' jerseys and Fiji in white 'FIJI AIRWAYS' jerseys, are on the field as the announcer introduces Ruby Tui. New Zealand players, some wiping sweat, walk off the field, interacting with staff. Ruby Tui notes New Zealand's successful night. A dejected Fiji player walks down the stadium steps. The camera focuses on the diverse Stadium Crowd clapping and waving flags. Ruby Tui praises the event. The scene transitions to the New Zealand 'All Blacks Sevens' team on a podium, receiving medals and trophies, including ORMOND (23), ROKOLISOA (4), and TITO-HARRIS (35). Commentators reflect on the players' emotions and the significance of the victory. One commentator highlights Cody Vaha's first tournament win at 19. The Stadium Crowd cheers and records the event. The podium celebration continues with Bill Beaumont and Alan Gilpin in the background. A man in a black suit presents a trophy as confetti falls. The announcer declares, "New Zealand, your 2023 Hong Kong Sevens winners!" The Stadium Crowd chants "New Zealand!" as the players, including Seoni Mal and Cody Vaha, celebrate, confirmed by an on-screen graphic declaring New Zealand as the 2023 HSBC Hong Kong Sevens Champions.

Cathay/HSBC Hong Kong Sevens.description: Branded prominently on the blue banners and backdrop, with logos for HSBC, Capgemini, and Cathay visible, establishing the event's identity and location, confirmed by the announcer as the '2023 Hong Kong Sevens'.

Stadium Crowd.description: A diverse group of fans and staff in the background, some wearing blue and white striped shirts with 'HKRU' logos and lanyards, audibly roaring, cheering, and chanting 'New Zealand!', while visually recording the event on smartphones below the podium.

New Zealand.description: Players in black 'All Blacks Sevens' jerseys with 'ALTRAD' branding, including ORMOND (23), ROKOLISOA (4), and TITO-HARRIS (35), along with Seoni Mal and Cody Vaha, seen receiving medals, shaking hands, and celebrating on a podium with officials and staff, raising their arms and embracing as they are awarded the championship trophy, officially declared by an announcer as the '2023 Hong Kong Sevens winners' and 'All Blacks Sevens' champions after a nine-year wait.

Fan in Sailor Costume.description: A fan wearing a blue sailor-themed outfit with a matching hat and sunglasses, holding a blue inflatable guitar, standing out in the crowd with a playful and festive appearance.

Fan with Blue and White Flag.description: A man in a black t-shirt with '23' and a circular logo, waving a large blue and white flag with both arms raised, showing intense support.

Fan in Blue and White Floral Shirt.description: A woman in a blue and white floral shirt, dancing and raising her arms in celebration, embodying the festive crowd energy.

Fan with Red and White Jersey.description: A close-up of a fan in a red and white jersey, shouting with excitement, capturing the raw emotion and energy of the crowd.

Fiji.description: Players in white jerseys with black and blue traditional patterns, 'FIJI AIRWAYS' branding, including one carrying a red and blue water bottle, walking down the stadium steps with a dejected demeanor, contrasting with the celebratory tone around the New Zealand team.

Official in Green Shirt.description: A man in a green polo shirt with a lanyard, actively shaking hands with players and smiling, likely a match official or event organizer.

Man in Red Polo Shirt.description: Wearing a red polo shirt with 'ALTRAD' branding, presenting a trophy to a player, likely part of the event's official staff or sponsor representative.

Woman in Blue and White Shirt.description: A woman in a blue and white striped shirt with a lanyard, standing beside the podium, smiling and clapping, possibly a team official or event staff member.

Fan in Red and White Jersey.description: A woman in a red and white jersey with a diamond logo, smiling and clapping enthusiastically, visible among the crowd on the podium level.

Press Staff.description: Individuals in the crowd holding up phones and cameras, capturing the celebration, indicating media presence and fan engagement.

raw_segments: The video opens with a wide aerial shot of the brightly illuminated Hong Kong Stadium at night, showcasing the expansive field marked with sponsor banners like 'CATHAY', 'HSBC', 'DHL', and 'Capgemini', and a central red arch displaying 'CATHAY/HSBC HONG KONG SEVENS'. Teams, including players in black 'All Blacks Sevens' jerseys and white 'FIJI AIRWAYS' jerseys, are gathered on the field as a male commentator begins, noting players will "walk up the steps" to "keep the traditions here." He then introduces co-commentator Ruby Tui, an Olympic medalist, as crowd cheers and applause are heard. The camera cuts to New Zealand players in black jerseys, some wiping sweat, walking off the field and interacting with staff, including player number 25 in conversation with a coach. Ruby Tui mentions she was "just waving to the Black Ferns out on the field there," confirming it's "been quite a night for New Zealand," who "have a lot to be proud of tonight" and will need a "new trophy cabinet" due to their hard work. The scene shifts to a dejected Fiji player in a white jersey carrying a red and blue water bottle, walking down the stadium steps. The camera returns to a wide shot of the field as players disperse, then focuses on the diverse crowd in the stands, clapping, cheering, and waving flags. Ruby Tui praises the "amazing" Cathay HSBC Hong Kong Sevens for putting on "such a good show," a sentiment reinforced by the continued crowd cheers. The atmosphere is celebratory yet winding down, with stadium lights casting a bright glow. The final frames capture the vibrant energy of the crowd, with a woman in a blue and white floral shirt dancing and a man waving a blue and white flag.

The video captures the jubilant aftermath of a rugby match, clearly identified as the Cathay/HSBC Hong Kong Sevens by prominent banners and sponsor logos like HSBC and Cathay. New Zealand players, wearing their black 'All Blacks Sevens' jerseys with 'ALTRAD' branding, including ORMOND (23), ROKOLISOA (4), and TITO-HARRIS (35), are seen on a raised podium, receiving medals and trophies. They shake hands, embrace teammates and officials, and exchange high-fives, appearing sweaty and exhausted but utterly elated. A commentator enthusiastically points out the scene, noting "all the people coming out and celebrating" this "great occasion." Another commentator reflects on the players' mindset, asking what's "going through their minds and their hearts" after achieving this victory "here in Hong Kong," referencing the Black Ferns' previous success. The first commentator then vividly describes the emotional experience of winning, thinking of "all your family" and the "hideous Sevens fitness trainings," concluding, "That was worth it, bro. That was well worth it for your country." He adds that "all their families will be watching and so proud of them." The second commentator highlights a specific player, "Cody Vai," noting it's his "first tournament, 19 years of age and he's won the blooming thing," a sentiment echoed by the first commentator who mentions "so many superstars back in New Zealand" who "dream about this." In the background, a diverse stadium crowd of fans and staff, some with 'HKRU' logos, cheer, clap, and record the event on their phones, contributing to the lively atmosphere with their audible celebratory noise. The bright, even lighting suggests an indoor or covered stadium setting, emphasizing the collective victory as the camera transitions from close-ups to wider shots of the team.

The jubilant podium celebration for the New Zealand 'All Blacks Sevens' team at the Cathay/HSBC Hong Kong Sevens unfolds, with commentators reflecting on the "huge moment for these boys" and their nine-year wait for this win. Players, including ORMOND (23), ROKOLISOA (4), and TITO-HARRIS (35), are seen in black jerseys, red-and-white sashes, and gold medals on a raised platform, identified by commentators as including Seoni Mal and Cody Vaha. They gather behind a blue banner displaying event and sponsor logos, while officials like World Rugby Chairman Bill Beaumont and Chief Executive Alan Gilpin are noted in the background. As a man in a black suit presents a large silver trophy, confetti falls, and the atmosphere intensifies. The stadium announcer's voice booms, declaring, "New Zealand, your 2023 Hong Kong Sevens winners!" This prompts a massive roar and "New Zealand!" chant from the stadium crowd, who are seen below the podium cheering, waving, and recording on smartphones. The announcer further confirms, "The All Blacks Sevens, a Hong Kong Sevens champions once again!" as the players cheer, raise their arms, and embrace, celebrating their victory, underscored by an on-screen graphic: 'NEW ZEALAND 2023 HSBC HONG KONG SEVENS CHAMPIONS'.

event_blocks.summary: The Cathay/HSBC Hong Kong Sevens culminates with the New Zealand 'All Blacks Sevens' team celebrating their victory on a podium amidst roaring crowd cheers, celebratory music, and exploding fireworks that illuminate the night sky above the Hong Kong Stadium. Players, including ORMOND (23), ROKOLISOA (4), and TITO-HARRIS (35), receive medals and shake hands with dignitaries, while commentators reflect on the team's continuity and DJ Forbes' past win. Graphics for 'DHL IMPACT PLAYER - MEN'S HONG KONG' and 'DHL IMPACT PLAYER - WOMEN'S HONG KONG' are displayed, highlighting top performers like Andrew Smith, Séraphine Okemba, and Reapi Ulunisau, with commentators praising their "massive weekends" and "outstanding" performances. The "New Zealand program" is lauded for its equally resourced men's and women's teams. Amidst the festive atmosphere, a fan in a dinosaur costume dances energetically, and the New Zealand team poses for photos with medals and scarves, with Sione Molia holding a trophy. Golden confetti rains down as the team celebrates their "hard work" and "outstanding" performance, culminating in a wide shot of the podium under a shower of confetti and fireworks, as a commentator exclaims, "What a moment!" The Hong Kong Sevens is declared to have "delivered once again."

Cathay/HSBC Hong Kong Sevens.description: Branded prominently on the red backdrop and podium, featuring logos of HSBC, DHL, Cathay, Capgemini, Bupa, Quality Healthcare, and Carlsberg, establishing the event's identity and sponsors. The event is the setting for the 'New Zealand' victory, with commentators providing context on the 'excellent cup finals' played and the overall 'outstanding' performance of the teams.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a wide shot of the New Zealand 'All Blacks Sevens' team, in their black 'ALTRAD' branded jerseys, celebrating on a podium at the Cathay/HSBC Hong Kong Sevens. Amidst the palpable excitement and crowd cheering, accompanied by celebratory music, players like ORMOND (23), ROKOLISOA (4), and TITO-HARRIS (35) receive medals and shake hands with dignitaries, while the backdrop prominently displays event sponsors. Commentators reflect on this significant moment, mentioning former player DJ Forbes, who 'knows what it feels like' to win, having been part of the New Zealand team that 'took it out' in 2014. They note his continued connection, stating 'a lot of his heart will be with these boys' and highlighting the strong continuity within the group. The camera briefly cuts to a bald man in a red and black 'KUKRI' polo shirt in the stands before returning to the podium where the New Zealand team continues to celebrate, waving and embracing as officials applaud. They then descend the stairs, interacting with fans and staff, one player proudly holding the championship trophy. An aerial view of the stadium follows, leading to a 'DHL IMPACT PLAYER - MEN'S HONG KONG' graphic overlay. Commentators announce that Andrew Smith from Ireland has 'won, well deserved,' as the graphic lists top performers with statistics, including Smith (70 points), Jordan Conroy (63), and Tuna Tuitama (61), while discussing the Sevens Academy and the atmosphere between the men's and women's programs.

The segment opens with an on-screen graphic for the 'DHL IMPACT PLAYER - MEN'S HONG KONG', displaying a leaderboard with top performers like Andrew Smith, Jordan Conroy, and Tuna Tuitama, as a commentator congratulates these "DHL Impact Players" for their "massive weekends." The scene then transitions to the 'DHL IMPACT PLAYER - WOMEN'S HONG KONG' graphic, listing Séraphine Okemba, Reapi Ulunisau, and Charlotte Caslick, with the commentator highlighting Okemba's "outstanding" performance. Amidst continuous crowd noise and upbeat music, the commentary shifts to praise the "New Zealand program" for its equally resourced men's and women's sevens teams, emphasizing their hard work. Visually, a New Zealand player in an 'All Blacks Sevens' jersey is seen interacting with fans and press, taking selfies near 'CATHAY/HSBC HONG KONG SEVENS' branded barriers, followed by the New Zealand team posing for a group photo on a podium with medals and scarves, celebrating the culmination of their "hard work." The festive atmosphere is further captured by a fan in a large orange dinosaur costume dancing energetically in the stands, directly noted by the commentator who exclaims, "Oh, the dinosaur saw it here first," adding that "the fans have brought the fun." The segment concludes with a wide-angle night view of the brightly lit stadium, filled with spectators and the Hong Kong cityscape in the background, as a second commentator remarks that "Hong Kong Sevens has delivered once again."

The video opens with a wide-angle view of the brightly lit Hong Kong Stadium at night, filled with spectators, as the New Zealand men's team, identified by commentators as the 'All Blacks Sevens,' celebrates their victory at the Cathay/HSBC Hong Kong Sevens. While commentators initially mention 'both the Black Ferns and the New Zealand men's team' being on the podium, the visual focuses solely on the men's team. Dressed in black 'All Blacks Sevens' jerseys with 'ALTRAD' branding, the team stands on a red podium adorned with event branding and sponsor logos. An announcer declares, 'Congratulations to our champions... New Zealand!', met with a roaring crowd. Commentators add, 'It's a blackout in Hong Kong,' referring to the All Blacks' dominance. The atmosphere is electric as fireworks explode above the stadium, illuminating the night sky with bursts of red, orange, and white sparks, accompanied by distinct 'fireworks' sounds of explosions, crackling, and whooshes. Commentators note, 'The pyrotechnics are underway,' and reflect on the teams' 'outstanding' performance, also referencing the 'Black Ferns' women's team's hard-fought win against the 'Aussie 7s women' and New Zealand's gold medal victory over 'Fiji.' Golden confetti rains down as the team, including Sione Molia holding a smaller trophy and smiling broadly, poses jubilantly with medals and scarves, raising their arms in triumph. The stadium crowd, including fans in colorful attire like a man in a blue sailor costume and a woman in a blue and white floral shirt dancing, cheers loudly, their excitement palpable through their roars and applause. The scene is a vibrant mix of celebration, pyrotechnics, and team pride, culminating in a final wide shot of the team on the podium under a shower of confetti and fireworks, as a commentator exclaims, 'What a moment!'

event_blocks.summary: The New Zealand women's rugby sevens team, the 'Black Ferns Sevens,' clad in black 'All Blacks Sevens' jerseys with 'ALTRAD' and 'adidas' branding, triumphantly celebrates their Cathay/HSBC Hong Kong Sevens victory. Golden confetti rains down as pyrotechnic flames erupt, illuminating the night sky with orange and white light, accompanied by the distinct sounds of fireworks and the roaring stadium crowd. Commentators emphasize the significance of the win, stating, "They will not forget this night. April 2nd, 2023 will live long in the memory for the New Zealand Rugby Sevens program." The players cheer, raise their arms, and embrace, with one player kissing the trophy, all while the packed stadium fans, including those in vibrant costumes like a dinosaur and a sailor, cheer loudly and record the electric atmosphere. As the celebrations continue, a commentator notes the team is preparing to perform their Haka, identified by Rubia as 'Ngā Roimata o Tūhoe,' meaning 'stand up,' a unique Haka gifted to the Sevens team around 2013 from Maropū in Rotorua. The minute opens with a male commentator celebrating the Black Ferns Sevens, emphasizing the special meaning of their win and their representation of "all your people back at home," concluding with "Congratulations to the Black Ferns Sevens, Wahine Toa." The team, initially solemn with heads bowed, launches into a powerful and emotional traditional haka, filling the stadium with intense, synchronized chanting, guttural shouts, and rhythmic stomps, clearly articulating phrases like "Ka mate! Ka mate! Ka ora! Ka ora!" The camera alternates between wide shots showing the full team in front of the red 'Cathay/HSBC Hong Kong Sevens' arch and close-ups highlighting their fierce expressions. The distant cheers of the large stadium crowd are audible, adding to the electric atmosphere as the team displays cultural pride, unity, and triumph. As the haka concludes with a final powerful chant, the stadium erupts in roaring crowd cheers and applause. A male commentator identifies Sarah Hirini leading the Black Ferns and mentions Ruby Tui performing the haka alongside her 'seven sisters,' praising the 'pretty great thing to see.' The team then gathers for a group photo, kneeling and standing with the championship trophy, their joy evident. From a high-angle view, the stadium is seen with confetti scattered across the grass, while the crowd continues to cheer. A female commentator reflects on the 'respect' for 'all the mahi of sevens,' calling it a 'very difficult sport.' The electric atmosphere, filled with cultural tradition, pride, and the joy of victory, is palpable throughout the packed stadium.
